rows.title: GIRLS' EDUCATION CHALLENGE (GEC) (Mercy Corps Scotland)
rows.description:
Supporting the Education of Marginalised Girls in Kailali (STEM)

1) Training marginalised girls to complete a full cycle of education. These girls provide academic and emotional support to some of the most marginalised girls, or Little Sisters, by Big Sisters, who mentor the girls through their schooling and act as positive role models. International volunteers train and support the Big Sisters, and work with them to mobilise commitment in communities and resources for the continuation of the scheme.
2) Providing nine-month "Bridge Courses" (preparatory classes and school enrolment support) to girls who have never been to school or who dropped out in Grades 1 to 3 and learning support classes to low performing girls to help keep them in school
3) Mentoring the Big Sisters through male and female adult champions from the local community facilitates negotiations with parents, adding credibility to the scheme.
4) Establishing gender-friendly school environments in schools that the projects marginalised girls attend.

rows.description: Sexual exploitation of children (SEC) is a grave violation of children’s rights and affects millions of children and youth annually, regardless of gender. No region, country or child is immune, though girls, boys and children with other gender identities face differing levels of risk to different manifestations of SEC depending on their intersectional vulnerability factors. It impacts heavily and long lastingly on their physical, emotional and mental well-being. It deprives children and youth of establishing healthy (sexual) relationships and from developing to the best of their potential. The SUFASEC programme is designed to combat SEC and will work in 12 countries in Latin America and South and South East Asia: Bangladesh, Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Dominican Republic, Guatemala, India, Indonesia, Laos, Nepal, Philippines and Thailand.

Nepal Earthquake 2015
rows.description: Two earthquakes in April and May caused massive damage in central Nepal killing 8 856 people destroying 543 000 homes and affecting 5.6 million people. The Nepal Red Cross Society (NRCS) launched an immediate relief effort with other 8 000 volunteers taking part. 29 Red Cross and Red Crescent directly supported the response as well as the IFRC and ICRC and in total the RCRC Movement has provided emergency shelter to over 500 000 people water and hygiene to over 500 000 and medical care to over 50 000. BRC launched an appeal for funds on the day of the earthquake and by 01/08/15 has raised 13.1million including a 4.1million from the DEC. Of this 5.9million has been committed to the emergency response efforts including: 1.4million of relief items sent from BRC stocks in Kuala Lumpur and Dubai including tarpaulins mosquito nets blankets and hygiene kits. 3.8million supporting IFRC emergency operations including shelter and cash distributions. Deployment of 11 delegates to be part of FACT the IFRC coordination unit overseeing the operation. Deployment of the Mass Sanitation Emergency Response Unit a four-person team that is providing safe drinking water clean toilets and hand washing facilities in Sindhupalchok the worst-affected district. As thousands struggle in the aftermath of this devastating earthquake we have launched the Nepal Earthquake Appeal.

rows.title: Nepal 2015 Earthquake Recovery
rows.description: BRC s Nepal Earthquake recovery programme in partnership with the Nepal Red Cross Society will support 50 communities (reaching around 140 000 people) across the Kathmandu valley in the areas of shelter livelihoods community health and WASH over the next 2 years. It will include training of masons carpenters and home owners in safer building techniques and restoring water supplies; cash grants and skills training to increase the income of families affected by the earthquake and training in community health first aid and psycho-social support.

rows.title: Nepal SURE programme
rows.description: The Strengthening Urban Resilience and Engagement ( SURE ) programme is a five-year DFID-funded multi-hazard urban risk reduction programme running from September 2016 until August 2021. Given emerging risks and hazard trends the programme continues engagement in the three districts of the Kathmandu valley and expanding further west into two additional districts. The programme aims to see increased action in target municipalities by citizens government and the Nepal Red Cross Society to reduce and respond to multi hazard risks increasing overall disaster resilience and enabling timely and effective response by the Nepal Red Cross Society. The targeted municipalities include six within the previously-engaged districts of the Kathmandu valley namely: Kathmandu sub-metropolitan city and Budhanilkantha municipality in Kathmandu district Bajrabarahi and Karyabinayak municipalities in Lalitpur district and Bhaktapur and Madhyapur Thimi municipalities in Bhaktapur district and two additional municipalities in the West and Far-West specifically Pokhara sub-metropolitan city and Dhangadhi sub-metropolitan city. Impact: Target municipalities have increased their disaster resilience and are supported by the Nepal Red Cross Society with timely and effective disaster response. Outcome: Targeted municipalities are increasingly taking action to reduce and respond to multi hazard risks through citizens government and the Nepal Red Cross Society. Output 1: Awareness and action in urban DRR for informed citizen voice and greater social accountability is increased enhancing DRR capacity in targeted municipalities. Output 2: DRR advocacy and support and strengthening of preparedness for response enhances the capacity of municipalities and is aligned to existing policies and structures. Output 3: NRCS is stronger and well organised in order to better respond to vulnerable communities and individuals. Output 4: NRCS and BRC actively learn with urban partners and external institutions to contribute to improved thematic and institutional knowledge and practice enabling efficient and effective programming with partners.

rows.title: Enhancing capacity for sustainable management of forests, land and biodiversity in the Eastern Hills (ECSM FoLaBi EH)
rows.description: 10. BenefitsThe project will contribute to the following outcomes of the GEF:·       BD-1-1 - Mainstream biodiversity across sectors as well as landscapes and seascapes through biodiversity mainstreaming in priority sectors; and·    LD-1-3 - Maintain or improve flows of ecosystem services, including sustaining livelihoods of forest-dependent people through Forest Landscape Restoration (FLR) through efforts targeted at policy and planning, knowledge generation and management and strengthening conservation-based livelihoods opportunities.Project intervention will help provincial government in improved land use planning at landscape level covering 3575 sq.km. area. In addition, all 34 local levels will have their land use plan ready which further guides them in judicial allocation of public finances for ecosystem restoration, degraded land rehabilitation and natural resource-based community enterprise development.Project support to the establishment and operation of knowledge management portal at province and federal level with access to data, information, maps and best practices ensures that budget allocation and implementation of land use plan follows informed decision making.Targeted CFUGs and households will benefit from incentive mechanisms that will strengthen community resilience to shocks and stresses, such as the COVID-19, through a) Improved food security resulting from sustainable and resilient production of agricultural and livestock products, and b) Enhanced livelihoods through participation in income generating activities based on increased and sustainable flows of ecosystem goods and services.Livelihood enhancement of targeted communities is one of the important aspects of the project intervention. Under this, support will be provided to 10 biodiversity enhancing pro-poor business services that will focus poorer section of community members and help 1000 entrepreneurs benefit from sustainable value chain intervention.As indicated in the project’s theory of change, implementation of biodiversity-sensitive landscape policies and land use plans will enhance stakeholder capacity to manage natural resources in a sustainable manner. In the long run, this contributes to ecosystem restoration, biodiversity conservation and achieving land degradation neutrality. Resilient ecosystems will help ensure a sustained flow of ecosystem services and contribute to improved livelihoods of forest-dependent communities.At project area level, landscape policy and land use plans supported by a knowledge management system, community-based ecosystem restoration and land management initiatives will demonstrate environmental and socio-economic benefits on the ground and facilitate scaling up. Economic benefits generated through the promotion of product- and service-based value chains engaging women, poorer sections of the community and private sector will incentivize communities to adopt biodiversity conservation friendly forestry and farming practices beyond the project duration.At province and national level, governments will benefit from enabling policy environment for integrated land management based on land use plans. In addition, enhanced capacities of the governments to manage data and information system for effective planning will have positive impact in other sectors as well for biodiversity conservation and sustainable land management.Through these changes at local, provincial, and national level, the project will contribute to achieve global environmental benefits in mainstreaming biodiversity and forest landscape restoration generating socio-economic benefits that strengthens resilience of communities to impacts of COVID-19.Specifically, the project supports the following (through component one and two):·       Field level reaffirmation of newly enacted policies and legal frameworks and their implications for enhancing capacity for sustainable management of forests, lands, and biodiversity.·       The application of new policies and regulations under the new governance structure will enable government institutions to identify policy practice gaps which will eventually facilitate policy reform and help to improve effectiveness on the ground.·       An analysis of existing policies and regulations in relation to institutional capacity, and resource need (human and financial) for effective policy implementation.·       Enable all three spheres of government to readjust institutional arrangements and make them compatible with the provisions of current policies and regulations.·       Support Local levels to identify the elements necessary for effective local policy and law formulation, including data needs, decision support systems and knowledge management (the exclusive power of local level), that are based on the real needs and aspiration of the people.Component 3 focuses on the implementation of community-based conservation and sustainable production, management and restoration practices at community and household level which strengthens biodiversity conservation and sustainable management of forest landscapes.Project interventions that focus on biodiversity enhancing activities by involving poorer sections of society in livelihood improvement opportunities through value chains and market linkages will help to sustainably increase incomes. The Project has accorded high priority to support women, poor and marginalized groups in value chains.Components 1 and 2 lay the foundation to creating an enabling environment at policy and programme level and Component 3 makes investment in enterprise promotion. These interventions together help to derive environmental as well as socioeconomic benefits by ensuring productive employment and decent work at local level.Decent Rural EmploymentIn rural Nepal, decent work is predominantly associated with livelihoods based on agriculture, livestock, and forests.Decent work can be considered to include “opportunities for work that is productive and delivers a fair income, security in the workplace and social protection for families, better prospects for personal development and social integration, freedom for people to express their concerns, organize and participate in the decisions that affect their lives and equality of opportunity and treatment for all women and men.”[1]  The project has a strong focus on improving rural livelihoods through community forestry and agriculture by generating and distributing livelihood benefits through commercial activity and linking smallholders to financial institutions and markets which will incentivize local communities to manage forests and other natural resources sustainably and improve community-level resilience.Table 12 provides a summary of how the project will support decent rural employment, based on the four pillars described in FAO’s guidance material.[2] Table 12 Project Support to Decent Rural EmploymentPrioritized Groups-    Small-scale farm and forest producers, including contributing family workers.-    Small-scale processors and aggregators of farm and forest products-    Women and youth within the above categories-    Specific vulnerable groups (e.g., land poor and landless people, disabled people, elderly people, and single-adult households)Pillar 1:  Employment-creation and enterprise-development-    Participatory analyses with vulnerable groups on specific rural employment issues related to farms and forests.-    Consider the impact of technology options on the number and quality of jobs created.-    Ensure that relevant groups within the targeted rural areas are involved effectively in consultations.-    Women and men small-scale farm and forest producers supported in accessing fair markets and sustainable value chains.-    Women and men small-scale farm and forest producers and supported in accessing training, financial services, and other productive assets, with priority to rural businesses owned by women and youth.-    Provide Market Analysis and Development training on how to develop viable market options for forest and farm products.-    Implement training for government agencies and project partners to enable them to undertake participatory approaches with local target communities and CFUGsPillar 2:  Social protection-    Asses, document and disseminate institutional innovations and good practices of organized collective action, including through CFUGs, with strong impacts on social protectionPillar 3:  Standards and rights at work-    Socially responsible agricultural and forest production supported, specifically to reduce gender and age-based discrimination.-    Promote compliance with national labour legislation in the rural areas.-    Address the constraints of women, youth, and other specific groups workers in getting organized, notably through community forestryPillar 4:  Governance and social dialogue-    Promote the inclusive participation of local people, particularly women.-    Support local communities in strengthening democratic organizations and networks of producers and workers, particularly in the informal food and forest economy.-    Build capacity of Province and Local Levels to empower forest and farm producers to organize into legalized associations and women to undertake leading positions.-    Undertake knowledge exchange events to refine and endorse the most promising approaches for climate-resilient forest and agricultural landscape management. [1] https://www.ilo.org/global/topics/decent-work/lang--en/index.htm[2]             FAO, 2010.  Rural Employment, Guidance Material #1:  Guidance on How to Address Decent Rural Employment in FAO Country Activities (2nd ed.).  http://www.fao.org/3/i1937e/i1937e.pdf

rows.title: Building National Capacities of Nepal to meet requirements of the Enhanced Transparency Framework of the Paris Agreement
rows.description: Capacity building of government at different levels (federal and provincial) and multi-stakeholders such as private sectors, academia, etc. through training and technical support would significantly improve the national capacities to transparently and regularly report on progress in implementing Nepal’s NDC targets  including monitoring and reporting on (a) GHG emissions or reductions attributed to a particular mitigation action; (b) climate-related support provided by the Government of Nepal or received from donors or the market in a form of finance and its impact in terms of technological enhancement, capacity building, or implementation of a certain action or as a result of an action taken in a particular sector of the economy; (c) policy support to identify alternatives to achieve climate resilient development. This would also address the capacity gap and dependency on international experts. Improvements in data collection, monitoring, analysis, reporting and validation will support policy decisions and their implementation, and in the longer run establish a low carbon development pathway for the country. GHG data and information generated will help government agencies to design appropriate measures to mitigate and adapt to climate change. Further, the required data collection, analyzes, monitoring and reporting would create new local jobs. In addition, transparent reporting and data-based decision-making will enhance climate resilience and coping strategies of the local people which increases their adaptive capacity and resilience to climate change generating less loss and hire income.

rows.title: Greening Transportation Infrastructure Development in Nepal: Terai Arc Landscape Case
rows.description: GRID-Nepal is committed to deliver socio-economic benefits in all project outputs by providing equal access to opportunities, resources, and benefits across the project’s activities. It also has a component in community engagement that fosters community ownership, engagement, and creation of income generation opportunities. While the project encourages diversity and inclusive decision making, the direct socio-economic benefits include:i) Strong Community Ownership and empowermentGRID Nepal will not only raise awareness and build capacities on green infrastructure issues but will also engage local communities in active site-level monitoring. This includes tracking solid waste management—both from point and non-point sources—near overpasses, observing wildlife sightings, and reporting incidents of human-wildlife conflict. These will be paid engagements, providing financial support to communities while fostering learning and behavioral change. By doing so, the project aims to empower local people, especially women, youth, and indigenous groups, to take on leadership roles and become custodians of the biodiversity and ecosystems in their area. This dual approach of livelihood support and environmental stewardship helps reinforce sustainable practices while creating inclusive income-generating opportunities.ii) Income generating activities and Improved LivelihoodsThe project will create income generating opportunities for the communities in TAL area, especially for women and youth by engaging oni) Youth leadership for biodiversity and income generation through activities such as engaging as a nature guide and other eco-tourism activities. This will not only empower youth and make them future leaders but help them be an active part of managing forests and grasslands in the area.ii) Provide technical and financial assistance to community forest users’ groups to engage local communities including women and youth to engage in activities that support biodiversity, for example ecotourism, especially around  Barandabar corridor. This will give communities a chance to use local knowledge to look at the environmental problems, and propose solutions that will benefit the communities, including youth, women and indigenous people financially while reducing the pressures on and increasing biodiversity and environmental benefits. The livelihood activities can also show how nature-based solutions can provide income-generating activities, diversify income sources, and improve working conditions for marginalized groups.iii) Promoting safety and well-being of the communitiesLocal park wardens, staff, rangers, and community members are often on the frontlines when dealing with safety hazards such as forest fires and human-wildlife conflicts. Recognizing their critical role, GRID Nepal is supporting these frontline actors through the provision of essential equipment for fire management, integrated within the broader habitat management plan. In addition, the project will implement targeted initiatives to mitigate human-wildlife conflict, including the installation of solar-powered electric fencing to protect vulnerable areas and communities from wildlife incursions. These interventions not only enhance the safety and resilience of both people and ecosystems but also reflect the project’s commitment to inclusive and community-driven conservation, improving Participation in Decision Making Grid Nepal will ensure the meaningful participation of both sexes and all ethnicities in understanding the policies and organizational rules to ensure equitable access and benefits from natural resources. The project promotes equal participation of women and men and indigenous people in the planning and decision-making process at the national and local levels.  Forums can serve as venues to convey issues and concerns of community forest users’ groups concerning human-wildlife conflict, fire control management, lack of diverse sources of livelihood, and lack of land tenure security. As a result, community based forest users’ groups can influence policy decisions and implementation strategies.iv) Improving Participation in Decision Making                       Grid Nepal will ensure the meaningful participation of both sexes and all ethnicities in understanding the policies and organizational rules to ensure equitable access and benefits from natural resources. The project promotes equal participation of women and men and indigenous people in the planning and decision-making process at the national and local levels. rows.title: NDC Partnership Support Unit and CAEP - BEIS
rows.description: The NDC Partnership (the Partnership) is a global coalition of countries and institutions collaborating to drive transformational climate action while enhancing sustainable development. The Partnership is working directly with developing country governments, mobilizing resources and expertise to support and accelerate their Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) implementation and increased ambition in updated NDC around the worlds. The Partnership is recognized as a neutral broker among stakeholders and a trusted partner, evidencing a strong governance structure, clear theory of change, and transparent strategy. Guided by a Steering Committee, the Partnership is comprised of developed and developing nations and international institutions and led by Co-Chairs from the Republic of Costa Rica and the Kingdom of the Netherlands. A Support Unit, which facilitates the work of the Partnership, has grown and consolidated to support its ambitious vision. As more governments seek support for turning NDCs into action, membership continues to grow considerably.

rows.title: AmplifyChange Fund Projects Implemented in Nepal
rows.description:
This IATI activity file represents the grantees receiving AmplifyChange funding for their projects in Nepal. Certain projects may have been anonymised in the data for safety and security reasons. AmplifyChange is a multi-donor fund that supports civil society organisations who advocate for improved sexual and reproductive health and rights (SRHR).  AmplifyChange offers both financial and capacity-building support to selected civil society organisations through a challenge fund model, where organisations submit applications in competitive funding rounds. AmplifyChange is managed by MannionDaniels in consortium with the African Women’s Development Fund and Global Fund for Women. 

AmplifyChange was tendered in 2013, established in 2014, and the first funding rounds were held in 2015.  The number of donors have increased since the beginning to a current total pot of approximately £90 million available for grant making (correct as of end-2017); the additional donor commitments have been extended to 2021. The donors include the Ministry of Foreign Affairs Denmark (Danida), the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Netherlands, the David and Lucile Packard Foundation, the William and Flora Hewlett Foundation, ViiV Healthcare, the Department of International Development (DFID) the Norwegian Agency for Development Cooperation (Norad), Swedish International Development Cooperation Agency (SIDA) working with the Swedish Association for Sexuality Education (RFSU), and one anonymous donor. Please refer to the parent IATI activity of this country activity for more information on the Fund.

AmplifyChange Fund support civil society organisations (CSOs) primarily in Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia, with some small grants being extended to organisations operating in Latin America, the Middle East and East Asia.  Applicants can apply across four grant types: 
1) Opportunity Grants: maximum 10,000 Euros over 1-year maximum
2) Strengthening Grants: between 40,000 – 100,000 Euros over 6 to 24 months
3) Network Grants: between 150,000 – 550,000 Euros over 2 years
4) Strategic Grants: between 550,000 to 2 million Euros over 2 years.

rows.title: Addressing Unmet Need for Family Planning among Excluded and Vulnerable Women in Nepal
rows.description: Increase in the use of contraceptives and family planning services in Nepal through: • Increased demand for family planning and safe abortion services amongst poor and hard-to-reach women, men and adolescents • Increased access to family planning services amongst poor and hard-to-reach women, men and adolescents • Improved policy environment to expand access to family planning and safe abortion services to poor and hard-to-reach women, men and adolescents

rows.title: Vineyard for Hope Pilot Project
rows.description: The aim of the pilot project is to develop volunteerism among young people in a place where it is practically non-existent and continue the process of teaching the truth of gender equality. The pilot project will take place at Dream School in Pokhara, Nepal where we wish to implement a youth-led sports activity. Students from year 11 and 12 will volunteer to train year 9 in handball. During the pilot project we want to secure its sustainability as we hope to expand the volunteering programme to other schools in the local community.

rows.title: Youth Forum for Change
rows.description: The pilot project aims to organize a national youth forum in Nepal, bringing together Nepalese youth scouts from all provinces with the purpose of electing and supporting a youth council. The project also involves the participation of Danish youth as participants/facilitators, fostering intercultural exchange and discussions on democratic processes within their respective organizations. The Danish participants will gain inspiration and ideas from the interactions with the Nepalese scouts, observing their youth programs and initiatives. Meanwhile, the Nepalese participants will develop action plans at provincial and national levels, empowering them to become local changemakers. The target groups of the project include Danish and Nepalese youth, as well as the partnering organizations involved.

rows.title: Preparatory study
rows.description: In our future partnership project, we wish to build the activities on our experiences from our last pilot project where mainly members of PSRN-sc went to rural areas of Nepal to teach young people in sexual and reproductive health and rights (SRHR). The objectives of this preparatory study are to examine the opportunities of educating in SRHR to the same extent as we succeed in the pilot project. Other than that we wish to expand the teaching material with information about mental health and furthermore to investigate the possibility of reaching additional rural areas of Nepal.

rows.title: Young Voices
rows.description: The partnership between Eureka Leo Club (LEO) in Kathmandu, Nepal, and the Youth Council of Mariagerfjord (MFU), Denmark. The main activities will revolve around ‘speaking up’ despite generational or cultural differences or inequality of opportunity. The collaboration aims to be a sustainable project empowering young people between the age of 16-28. Nepal is relatively new-democratic, whereas in Denmark we have only lived in a democracy. We will explore activities that can inspire and empower young people to participate and take a stand, in order to feel a part of society and to prevent that we don’t take democracy for granted. In addition, the preparatory study will strengthen our relations and ensure a jointly written pilot project.

rows.title: Sexual, reproductive og mental health in Tanahun, Nepal
rows.description: The aim of the Partnership Project is to increase knowledge on SRHR (Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights) and mental health among Nepalese students from grade 7, 8, 9 and 11 of Tanahun district through teaching, formation of student clubs and advocacy work The primary target group will be involved in various numbers of activities during the Partnership Project, which include interactive lectures, workshops, and peer-to-peer transfer of knowledge to other students All activities will be based on a baseline questionnaire conducted in the beginning The project will work together with local stakeholders and persons of interest to ensure sustainability and local support The project will furthermore focus on capacity building of PSRN-sc and IMCC Nepal, and the strengthening of our partnership

rows.title: Partnership Project-Junkiri
rows.description: A partnership project is a large project (up to 750.000 DKK). A partnership project allows you to work strategically with children and youth activities, organizational development and capacity building, and advocacy. A partnership project is expected to have a significant impact which will last after the project support has ended.Before writing the application, go through the guidelines for DUFs International Pool. Please pay special attention to the requirements for Partnership Projects (page 32-33), the change triangle (page 14-15) and the three crosscutting requirements (page 24). Your project will be evaluated based on the 6 evaluation criteria(page 42). You are welcome to contact DUF for advice on designing an effective Partnership Project in accordance with DUFs guidelines.The application should include a thorough context and problem analysis and a clear and realistic description of how your project addresses the challenges identified. Include only relevant and necessary information, and make sure that the total application does not exceed 15 pages (using Spectral front 10, excl. front page andsignatures). Please read the instructions and guiding questions under each sub headline carefully. By following the instructions, you will be able to describe all matters required in the guidelines. The instructions and guiding questions should be deleted before submitting the application. For partnership projects the following annexes must be included:- Please attach the budget, the partnership agreement, and a time plan, and if needed other relevant information not included in the application form, as annexes. The budget must be done in DUFs budget format.

rows.title: Democracy, rights and young influence in Nepal
rows.description: The project empowers Nepalese youth by promoting democratic values and active participation in decision-making. In partnership with KFUM-Spejderne i Danmark, Nepal Scouts will focus on intercultural exchange, leadership development, and community engagement. Key activities include leadership training, educational materials on democracy and human rights, and center exchanges. Targeting young scouts, the project strengthens their role in local and national governance through youth councils and peer-to-peer learning, where youth educate youth across provinces and in all 700 local groups.

rows.title: Youth Leader Exchange in Relation to IMCC Nepal and PSRN-sc?s Partnership Project on SRH(R) and Mental Health for adolescents in Nepal
rows.description: The Youth Leaders (YLs) will be attached to the partnership project between IMCC Nepal and PSRN-sc on Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights (SRHR) and mental health The purpose of the Youth Leader exchange (YLE) is to support the project objectives and activities, and to strengthen the capacity and communication within the partnership The YLs will have a special focus on educating members of PSRN in different workshops, and an ongoing evaluation of how the project is going in the ?new? project-district; Tanahun, where our teaching, empowerment and advocacy work will focus on students from grades 7, 8, 9 and 11 The YLs (two from each organization) will work for 3 months during the two year project The implementation of Youth Leaders will serve as an important support function in the project targeting adolescents in a rural district of Nepal

rows.title: Youth Leader Exchange 2023
rows.description: The Youth Leaders (YLs) will be attached to the partnership project between IMCC Nepal and PSRN-sc on Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights (SRHR) and mental health. The purpose of the Youth Leader exchange (YLE) is to support the project objectives and activities and to strengthen the capacity and communication within the partnership. The previous YL’s' exchange to Nepal and project activities was cancelled, due to the COVID-19. We consider YLE as a great opportunity for our partnership to develop and for the motivation and communication to flourish again. The YLs will have a special focus on capacity building of members of PSRN in different workshops, and an ongoing evaluation of how the project is going in the ‘new’ project-district; Tanahun, where our teaching, empowerment, and advocacy work takes place. The duration of the YL exchange will be 3 months in Nepal, but the YLs will remain active members of our organizations after the exchange

rows.title: Inclusive and Resilient Community with Empowered and Engaged People. Youth Leaders.
rows.description: The youth leader exchange will support the implementation of the Inclusive and Resilient Community with Empowered and Engaged People (ICE) that starts in May 2023 (annex 11). The project is implemented by Nepal Red Cross Society (NRCS) and Danish Red Cross (DRC) in rural municipalities in Lamjung District, Gandaki Province, Nepal. The DRC and NRCS has a long-standing partnership and have implemented projects in the Lambjung District for the past 15 years. The ICE project has a strong focus on empowering youth and vulnerable groups by capacity-building them to position themselves as agents of change in their families and communities and through improving their socio-economic status. Unemployment is a major challenge for youth in Nepal due to the caste system and inadequate economic growth. The youth leader exchange will support the project by strengthening the capacity of key NRCS youth volunteers to support local youth in Lamjung in building personal capacities and initiate youth-led projects to enhance their livelihood.

rows.title: Inclusive and Resilient Community with Empowered and Engaged People, Youth Leader Exchange Project (YLEP2)
rows.description: This youth leader exchange project, YLEP2, is the second phase of youth leader exchange which will support the youth activities in the project “Inclusive and Resilient Community with Empowered and Engaged People” (ICE) in Lamjung, Nepal. This project builds on learnings from the first exchange in 2023 and it aims to empower youth by enhancing their capacity and enabling them to position themselves as change agents in their families and communities. The porject will strengthen the capacity of Nepal Red Cross youth volunteers to support local youth in Lamjung district by building personal capacities and initiating youth-led projects to enhance their livelihood. The key activities are training of youth as peer facilitators in the core DRCY methods ‘Life Skills’ and ‘Sprint’ (activity kickstarter), and the roll-out of sessions in schools and community youth circles facilitated by the peer facilitators and supported by the youth leader team.

rows.title: Youth Leader Exchange in Relation to Project Junkiri
rows.description: The Youth Leaders (YLs) will be connected to the Junkiri project, a partnership project between the Danish organization IMCC Nepal and the Nepalese organization Physicians for Social Responsibility Nepal Student Chapter (PSRN-SC) concerning Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights (SRHR) and mental health. The purpose of the Youth Leader Exchange (YLE) is to strengthen the partnership between the organizations by facilitating capacity building and thereby supporting the project's activities. Just as important, the YLE allows easier communication and the opportunity to work with more rapidly growing results, which is also essential for motivation and sustainability. Recently, issues regarding bank transfer has caused a six-month delay in the beginning of the project. Therefore, the YLE will be supporting PSRN-SC in the process of kickstarting activities. The YLE will last 3 months in Nepal, but the YLs will continue to remain active in the organizations after the exchange.

rows.title: Youth Leader Exchange Program in the partnership between Nepal Scouts and KFUM-Spejderne i Danmark
rows.description: The exchange programme between NS and KS supports the ongoing partnership project, which focuses on ensuring long-term youth involvement in NS, based on the principle of youth teaching youth. The project pursues this goal by delivering local and provincial training for young scout leaders in key topics such as democratic values, mental health, and local changemaking. YLs in the exchange will act as cornerstones for reaching local groups, conducting trainings, and starting a domino effect whereby they teach others, driving continuous youth empowerment in both Nepal and Denmark and helping to break intergenerational barriers.

rows.title: Young voices - NNDSWO // Mariagerfjord Youth Council
rows.description: With this application we are applying for a Partner Identification in Nepal with NNDSWO. We have been in contact with each other for the last year, and we do have an idea about what a potential future project could look like, as well as a sense of mutual ambitions on behalf of our respective groups - but we still need to work on our foundation, and most importantly match our expectations in a partnership.   When we first got in contact last year after a conference about international partnerships, we matched because both our organizations work hard on creating an environment where young people feel heard and where they can express themselves in democratic ways. Both organizations aspire young people to be a part of a community where their participation matters and where they can grow into strong and responsible citizens. All of this happens while they participate in or organize projects for their peers, and that?s where we see a huge potential with an international project like this; what existing projects can we learn from, what can we create with this target group and how can we build an even stronger group of young people in both Nepal and Denmark.

rows.title: Partnership with Nepal Scouts
rows.description: KFUM-Spejderne in Denmark has a long tradition of longstanding partnerships with foreign scout associations. We have now had a longer search for a possible new partner in South East Asia, and we found a match in capacity and intentions in Nepal Scouts.  We have had a few online meetings, and we have agreed to clarify our possibilities in a physical meeting. We, therefore, wish to send a group from Denmark including 3 persons from the planning team to Nepal, and we will then meet with members of Nepal Scouts to set up guidelines for possible future cooperation.  We have realised that exchanging values, wishes, and experiences with a possible new partner demands both time and presence, and this we believe can be an option through this Partner Identification trip. We also need to clarify our common understanding of the frame and the options in a possible future partnership, and we find a physical meeting is needed.

rows.title: Folkemødet - The Peoples Democratic Festival
rows.description: The aim of the Network Activity is for PSRN-SC and IMCC Nepal to participate and engage at Folkem?det - The People?s Democratic Festival 2019, together. We believe that this is a golden opportunity for the selected members from both organizations to learn, interact, network and build capacity for the benefit of the partnership and our future projects. The participants could furthermore get inspired on how to do advocacy and be empowered to become advocates themselves. A team of five participants, two from PSRN-SC and three from IMCC Nepal, will participate in this meeting between people and politicians to debate current political issues. The participants will also facilitate two events at Folkem?det - The People?s Democratic Festival 2019, in cooperation with some other non-profit organizations.

rows.title: Folkem�det - The People's democratic festival 2025
rows.description: The aim of the Network Activity is for PSRN-SC and IMCC Nepal to together participate and engage in Folkem�det - The People�s Democratic Festival 2025 in Denmark. We believe that this is an opportunity for the selected members from both organizations to learn, network, generate innovative ideas and capacity build for the benefit of our partnership, our current partnership project as well as future projects. By engaging in discussions, workshops, and debates, PSRN-SC and IMCC Nepal aim to enhance their advocacy skills, present their partnership projects to stakeholders, and explore sustainable approaches to health and social welfare. A team of two participants from PSRN-SC and two from IMCC Nepal will participate in this network activity. The participants will also facilitate an event at Folkem�det, concerning some of the work and topics that the partnership is centering through different projects. This event will potentially be completed in cooperation with other branches of IMCC. Before Folkem�det, we will review the event and discuss our expectations and goals. Afterwards, we'll collectively evaluate the outcomes and identify areas for improvement in our partnership and future activities.

rows.title: Partnership development in Relation to IMCC Nepal and PSRN-sc’s Partnership Project on SRH(R) and Mental Health for adolescents in Nepal
rows.description: The Partnership Development project will contribute to strengthening the foundation of the partnership between PSRN-sc and IMCC Nepal. It will take place in Nepal. We wish to create a broader inclusion in the partnership, aiming to make it more sustainable and democratic. We also aim to make our partnership and our work within this more transparent - also to the rest of PSRN-sc and for our future work together as partners. Our objective is to include members from at least four faculties in MEC.

rows.title: Development Visit From Nepal
rows.description: The project “Development visit from Nepal Scouts” aims to actualize the experiences we gained during the pilot project "Youth Forum for Change." Here, we learned the importance of being physically together when planning, brainstorming, and connecting. It has proven crucial for the process, and we are confident that this project would be a catalyst for the further development of the overall partnership, allowing many more scouts to engage in the partnership. During the pilot project visit, we developed a timeline for 2024 and partly for 2025 and 2026. When finished, we all agreed that this would have been close to impossible to complete online. Furthermore, our partnership has now finished the initial phase, allowing us as partners to be more honest, direct, and understandable to each other. Therefore, with representatives for the youth, scout centres, activity developers, and the project leaders, we feel well assured that this project/visit will play a key role in ensuring that the dreams encapsulated in the timeline will actually be realised successfully.

rows.title: Project development, project Junkiri
rows.description: The objective of Project Junkiri is to enhance understanding of SRHR (Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights) as well as mental health among students in grades 7, 8, 9, and10 in 3 di?erent districts of Nepal. The increased knowledge of this subject will help in eradicating the taboos and embarrassment associated with SRHR and mental health. This will be achieved through workshops by PSRN-sc volunteers, the establishment of student clubs at the local school, and advocacy e?orts. The primary focus will be on engaging the target group in a variety of activities, including interactive lectures, workshops, and the transfer of knowledge among peers. All initiatives will be tailored based on initial baseline questionnaires. Collaborative e?orts with local stakeholders and individuals of interest will be undertaken to ensure sustainability and gather local support. Additionally, the project will emphasize the capacity building of PSRN-SC and IMCC Nepal, aiming to strengthen our partnership.

rows.title: Nepal Floods Joint Response
rows.description: This transitional recovery project has been designed as a follow up of ongoing response projects that is being implemented by CARE in consortium with other organizations in Bardiya, Banke, Surkhet and Dang districts of mid west Nepal to address the dire need of flood and landslide affected population and to prepare full fledge recovery and the rehabilitation phase.

rows.title: DFID RRF Nepal Earthquake
rows.description: The proposed intervention will focus on: Shelter assistance consisting of a combination of emergency shelter kits, toolkits and, if markets allow, unconditional cash transfers. Household non-food items as well as Gender sensitive dignity kits. Protection, IEC and Gender mainstreaming: Teams of Information Volunteers (IV)[1] will deliver messages verbally and through participatory activities.

rows.title: DFID Nepal Shelter Response
rows.description: Winterisation support for 35,000 vulnerable women, girls, boys and men to improve emergency/temporary shelters? resistance to winter weather in three of the most earthquake-affected Districts, and supporting safe spaces for women and girls.

rows.title: DFID Earthquake WASH Nepal
rows.description: This Rapid Community WASH Recovery project aims to meet the ongoing WASH needs of vulnerable and marginalised groups in disaster-affected districts in order to support their recovery. Implemented through an experienced consortium of INGOs offering high value for money with existing presence, experience, resources and relationships in the target areas, the project seeks to support local and national government recovery plans to build back better and more resilient and equitable water schemes and WASH infrastructure. Using a highly participatory and accountable approach and specific focus on vulnerable and marginalised groups, the project will build local capacity to plan, construct and manage safe, adequate and resilient water schemes, as well as increasing local knowledge to promote positive WASH behaviours.

rows.title: DFID Nepal Safe Justice
rows.description: This project aims to build trust between poor and marginalised communities, in particular vulnerable groups within those communities, and formal and informal justice providers, in order to enable marginalised populations to access fair and equitable justice.

rows.title: MM Reconstruction Nepal
rows.description: The overall objective is as to support the recovery of people in disaster-affected areas through the improvement of physical WASH facilities; increasing the promotion of safe personal, household and community hygiene and sanitation practices in order to reduce the prevalence of water borne diseases; improve quality of life and increase the availability of information on water sources and other related WASH components in the target VDCs.

rows.title: DFID (MM) Purnima COVID WASH
rows.description: CARE Nepal, as part of our larger COVID-19 response program has planned to implement the Purnima COVID WASH Response initiative to reduced risk of potential COVID 19 outbreak through improved WASH facilities

rows.title: DFID NERF Nepal (COVID19)
rows.description: The project aims to reach the most vulnerable to mitigate the secondary impacts of Covid and maintain resilience into monsoon season. The response will target 4 priority sectors

rows.title: Nepal Urban Resilience Program (NURP)
rows.description: The aim of the Nepal Urban Resilience Program (NURP) is to support national and local government in strengthening the resilience of growing urban areas to natural disasters through improved building, planning and services. ICF and its consortium are providing technical assistance support to the Ministry of Urban Development (MoUD) and municipal governments to achieve the following objectives: • Strengthen the disaster resilience of up to 7 rapidly urbanising areas and to facilitate learning from these urban areas to enable replication in other locations; and • Strengthen national policy development, implementation and monitoring in relation to disaster resilience, as well as stronger coordination and collaboration between the Government and development partners nationally.

rows.title: PO 3208 - Technical Assistance to the Government of Nepal on Post Earthquake Reconstruction Planning
rows.description: The purpose of this Technical Assistance assignment is to support District Governments in their efforts to develop comprehensive five-year reconstruction and recovery strategies. In doing so, the support should reinforce district capacity to coordinate the multiple actors engaged in recovery from both local and international public, NGO and private investments. The Reconstruction TA project is planned to support the districts of Nuwakot, Dhading and Rasuwa – three districts the western portion of the area most affected by the 2015 earthquakes in Nepal.

rows.title: A Window to Nepal
rows.description: The main message for our project is to emphasize the importance and inclusiveness in global development cooperation. As for our case it will be in Lamjung, Nepal.  Target Group: Our primary target audience is Danish youth from age of 20-29 interested in global development agenda. The target group will see content from the community in Lamjung, Nepal, where we will document activities through photos and videos for social media, particularly on Facebook and Instagram.   We will be writing stories, newsletters, and video editing, benefiting both local and national organizations (DUF, DRCY and NRCS) and enhancing our awareness-raising campaign. It will enable us to create visually appealing content and write stories/blogposts about our project.

rows.reporting: The Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria
rows.title: Nepal National Stretegic Plan 2010-2015-Implementation of Stop TB Strategy - Nepal (16-Jul-2013-15-Jul-2015)
rows.description: Tuberculosis (TB) remains one of the most prevalent infectious diseases and significant public health problems in Nepal and continues to pose serious threat to the health of the population and development of the country. Global targets for TB control have been achieved by the Program consistently over the past several years and Nepal has achieved the Millennium Development Goal of reducing the prevalence of TB by 50 per cent. This National Strategic Application grant aims to build on the gains made during the last 13 year of DOTS implementation and ensure the quality assured comprehensive implementation of the Stop TB Strategy.  The goal of the Program is to reduce the mortality, morbidity and transmission of TB until it is no longer a public health problem in Nepal. The Program is targeting districts with low TB case detection rates; TB and multidrug-resistant TB (MDR-TB) patients and their close contacts; patients suffering from respiratory illnesses; and public health care providers. This Program consolidates the Round 7 and Round 4 TB grants and the approved National Strategy Application.

rows.title: Teaching the unreached: Increasing enrolment and retention of children in primary education in remote regions of Nepal
rows.description: This project providers over 8,500 out of school children in 63 remote and marginalised communities of Nepal with access to an education. UWS will also recruit and train 143 local people from the communities to become Teachers in each of the schools and set up 63 School Support Committees to provide ongoing governance and management to each of the schools.RECIPIENT COUN

rows.title: Improving participation in an inclusive, quality education for girls living in small, remote & marginalised communities of Cambodia and Nepal.
rows.description: The key objective of the project is to provide approx. 14,000 girls in 211 remote qnd rural communities in Cambodia and Nepal with the opportunity to effectively participate in Primary Education. UWS will develop and train 1,452 teachers in gender responsive pedagogy, and equip them with the skills to deliver an inclusive, quality education. This will be supported by a range of interventions and advocacy which will tackle specific barriers to girls education at multiple levels and improve participation.

rows.reporting: University of the West of England
rows.title: NIHR GHPSR Development Award: Identification of Research Priorities for Road Safety in Nepal
rows.description: This activity is a research study to achieve consensus on the priorities for research on road safety in Nepal, using the framework of the WHO 5 Pillars of road safety.

rows.reporting: University of the West of England
rows.title: Global Health Research Programme: A 'safe Systems' Approach for Enabling Traffic Injury Prevention In Nepal (SAFETrIP Nepal)
rows.description: The objectives of our four work packages are:Work Package 1:• To identify existing policies and programmes at local, provincial and national levels of government in Nepal that can be linked to evidence-based recommendations for safer road systems• To understand the current reach and implementation of these policies and programmes• To identify the barriers and enablers to further implementation of these policies and programmes• To develop a toolkit for those working at local and provincial government level to help them improve road safetyWork Package 2:• To identify the factors that influence how bus transport is currently provided in Nepal, and how those factors affect bus safety• To establish how these factors affect the current provision of bus transport and which factors can best be used as levers towards safer buses and bus services• To identify which established, cost-effective, interventions are needed to shift current practice and policy• To generate agreement across sectors on recommendations to make bus travel saferWork Package 3:• To determine the health, social and economic impacts of being injured in a road traffic crash in Nepal.• To understand the facilitators and barriers to optimal handover of road traffic injured patients from prehospital carers to hospital emergency department staff• To establish stakeholder agreement on a best practice care pathway for road traffic injured patients to be used in hospital emergency departmentsWork Package 4:• To deliver five, seven-week online good data governance courses between 2023 and 2026• To develop and make available training materials that enable the safe collection, use and storage of research data• To strengthen the capacity for peer-to-peer learning on good data governance within and between low and middle-income countries.

rows.title: Women in climate resilient Agri Systems for enhanced Nutrition Security
rows.description: Overall objective:Contribution to fostering climate-resilient local food systems and empowering women in the mountain district of AchhamOutcome:2,500 predominantly women-headed marginalized farmer households in two communities in Achham district have improved their nutritional situation and are empowered to exercise their rights in the areas of nutrition and climate change.

rows.title: Civil society for nature-based, local agri-food-systems (CS4FS)
rows.description: Direct target groups: 5.000 marginalized households will directly benefit from the project (approx. 26.610 persons). The selection of the households will be based on their socio-economic status such as small holder farmers

rows.title: Earthquake response 2023
rows.description: On Nov 3, 2023, earthquake with a magnitude of 6.4 struck the western region of Nepal, specifically in the Karnali province. The epicenter was located in Ramidanda, Jajarkot district. Several aftershocks occurred, causing significant distress among the local population. These aftershocks (as of 08.11.23) have compelled residents to evacuate their homes and shelter in open areas, with experts warning of the possibility of a major aftershock in the affected areas. It was reported as 158 people have died, with 13,705 households affected and 12,060 households displaced. 9,483 houses have been fully destroyed and a further 19,286 partially destroyed. (Red Cross SitRep).Nepal Earthquake Response project is implemented in partnership with RRN Nepal in Jajarkot and Rukum districts of Nepal.

rows.title: Earthquake response - German Embassy
rows.description: Participants will be selected based on the assessment report and in close coordination with local government.  The specific needs of the highly vulnerable or socially excluded populations will be assessed through focus group discussion, data of the Initial Rapid Assessment (and detail assessment report will be used, coordination with the local government). Assistance packages will be specifically designed to accommodate the different needs or men, women, girls, and boys. Additionally, both Rural Reconstruction Nepal's(Implementing partner ) links to the target communities, and their expertise in supporting hard to reach areas, will help ensure that particularly vulnerable people of any gender, in addition to people with disabilities will have access to the support. Rural Reconstruction Nepal has demonstrated its capacity to deliver gender and age-sensitive assistance. The total targeted participants is 2000 Households.

rows.title: Skill Up! Unlocking success: Empowering vulnerable youth for sustainable perspectives in Nepal
rows.description: Overall Objective: The economic stability and decent income generation of vulnerable young people have improved through profitable employment or self-employment, resulting in increased food and nutrition security.Outcome (Project Objective):Young vulnerable people use the acquired (labour) market relevant competencies and the provided access to necessary resources & labour market linkages to secure (self-) employment opportunities.

rows.title: Digital Innovations for Zero Hunger 3
rows.description: Enhance food security in India, Malawi, Nepal, and Uganda through scalable digital innovations. The Project aim to digital literacy, scaling quality data collection to train AI for the Child Growth Monitor app, expand access of farmers to Digital Agriculture Solutions through leveraging synergies across technology advancements, AI, collaborative content creation, and business model testing, as well as facilitating cross-country exchange sessions. The project aims to reach 13,050 children under the age of 6 yr, 460,000 smallholder farmers through our implementing partners: ACE, FUM, CSONA, PALM, Li-Bird and DSC

rows.title: Food security&resilient livelihoods, focus on earthquake affected families through civil society strengthening - SUSTAIN
rows.description: Overall Objective: Contribution to improved livelihoods in local food systems for resilient communities in Jajarkot and Rukum West Districts. Project Objectives/ Outcomes: 1,750 earthquake affected households in Jajarkot and Rukum West have improved their food and nutrition security, and the civil society and local governments promote climate resilient and nutrition-sensitive agriculture and economic resilience programsIndicators: The targeted households (1,750) have at least 10 months of food sufficiency in their house by the end of the project. (MAHFP)70% (210 out of 300) of trained women and youth households increase their annual cash income by 20% by the end of the projectClimate resilience is anchored in the local development plans and the share in the budget is at least 5% by the end of the project

rows.title: Strong (women) smallholders and CSAs of Nepal’s western mountain regions for a resilient local food system (EMPOWER)
rows.description: Nepal’s mountain food system faces severe challenges due to loss of agrobiodiversity, out-migration, feminization of agriculture, low productivity, and growing climate risks. Poor diets, gender barriers, and weak local solutions further limit resilience.Target Group & Area: 2,000 households in three municipalities of Bajura and Mugu.Overall Objective: Empower women smallholders and CSAs to build resilient local food systems. Key Outputs:Enhanced capacities on agrobiodiversity, climate-resilient, and nutrition-sensitive practices.Strengthened local markets for safe and nutritious foods.Empowered CSAs and networks advocate for the right to food. Partners: LI-BIRD, KCDC, FWEAN.

rows.title: Supporting the Nepalese government in achieving SDG#6 through system-strengthening approaches.
rows.description: (1) Micro level (community): The project supports WASH services in five municipalities of Saptari and Siraha districts, Madhesh Province, covering 180,367 people (37,767 households). It targets marginalized and vulnerable households, WSUCs, sanitation workers, students, teachers, and CBOs. Beneficiaries will gain from climate-resilient water supply schemes, capacity building for WSUCs and water boards, functional faecal sludge and waste management facilities, school WASH improvements, and local enterprise promotion. (2) Meso level (province & local): Five local administrations with elected representatives and technical departments are targeted. Primary targets include provincial representatives, officials from MoEIWS, MoHP, MoEC, MoFE, local government representatives, DWSSM, and district CAN. Interventions include capacity building, technical support (e.g., WASH Act implementation, NWASH MIS, guidelines, advocacy), and knowledge products for planning and resource mobilization. Secondary targets are other municipalities in the province. Direct: 130 officials, 50 private actors; indirect: 200 officials, plus broader provincial stakeholders.(3) Macro level (national/federal): Provincial and federal officials, elected representatives, parliamentarians, and development partners are targeted. WASH officials, MoWS, DWSSM, and civil society actors will benefit from knowledge products, technical support, and standards development. Direct: 55 officials, 50 civil society actors; indirect: 100 officials and civil society actors, plus the national population through policy, guideline, and investment initiatives.

rows.title: Emergency Relief Support Project in Ilam
rows.description: In October 2025, Ilam district was hit by floods and landslides, causing 37 deaths, 1 missing, and widespread destruction of homes, infrastructure, and livelihoods across eight municipalities. Thousands were displaced, lacking shelter, food, health, and sanitation, while agriculture and food stocks were destroyed, increasing food insecurity and disease risk. This project targets 350 vulnerable households, including women, Dalits, and Indigenous groups, providing emergency food, shelter items, warm clothing, and hygiene kits, with post-distribution monitoring and community engagement to ensure timely relief and accountability.

rows.title: Restoration and Seismic Strengthening of the Early 20th-Century Gaddi Baithak in Kathmandu Durbar Square
rows.description: Built in 1907 as an addition to the Hanuman Dhoka, the royal residence of Nepali kings, and damaged during the April 2015 Nepal Earthquake, the Gaddi Baithak is one of the most prominent monuments in Kathmandu Durbar Square. This project involves the restoration and seismic strengthening of the building.

rows.title: Emergency Protection of the Kaiser Library Collections and Post-Earthquake Stabilization of the Late-19th-Century Kaiser Mahal in Kathmandu
rows.description: Since the late 1960s, the Kaiser Mahal has been home to the Kaiser Library, one of Nepals oldest libraries. This project, which involves emergency stabilization of the south wing of the building and the protection of books, maps, manuscripts, furnishings, and other objects in the library, directly supports the U.S. embassys efforts to strengthen U.S. cooperation with the government and people of Nepal on disaster risk reduction and response.

rows.title: Restoration of the 17th- and 18th-Century Patan Royal Palace, Phase 2
rows.description: To support the continuation of work begun under a previous Ambassadors Fund grant to restore the Patan Royal Palace, one of South Asias most intact historic urban royal ensembles. Major earthquakes in 1833 and 1934 and decades of neglect and disuse have left their mark on the palace, most of which dates from the 17th and 18th centuries. This phase includes the restoration of the late 18th-century Bahadur Shah palace wing.

rows.title: Documentation of the 17th-Century Kartik Nach
rows.description: To support the documentation of the 17th-century Kartik Nach, a month-long dance originally commissioned for and performed at the former Patan Royal Palace. The dance showcases Nepals ethnic diversity, yet with the last full performance of the dance in 1949 only a very few performers survive who understand the dance in its entirety. This project includes the comprehensive documentation of the Kartik Nach and the transmission of the tradition through training workshops.

rows.title: Structural Assessment and Restoration of the South Tajelu Temple and Other Structures at Patan Royal Palace Affected by the April 2015 Nepal Earthquake
rows.description: The April 2015 Nepal Earthquake caused extensive damage to historic monuments within Nepals Kathmandu Valley World Heritage site. This project involves the structural assessment of the South Taleju Temple and other structures at the Patan Royal Palace restored previously with AFCP support.

rows.title: Conservation of the 17th-Century South Taleju Temple of Patan Royal Palace
rows.description: The floating roofs of Patan Royal Palaces Taleju temples are unique characteristics of the Newa architecture of the Kathmandu Valley. A continuation of work completed under previous Ambassadors Fund grants, this project involves the conservation and seismic strengthening of the palaces South Taleju Temple.

rows.title: Conservation of the 17th-19th-Century Hanuman Dhoka Durbar (Palace) in Kathmandu
rows.description: The complex, situated in the historical core of Kathmandu, was the residence and administrative center of kings from ancient times, symbolizing the height of medieval arts and architecture in Nepal. This project addresses conservation needs of the Palace Complex caused by the 2011 earthquake damage.

rows.title: Structural Assessment and Restoration of Structures in Kathmandu Durbar Square Affected by the April 2015 Nepal Earthquake
rows.description: The April 2015 Nepal Earthquake caused extensive damage to historic monuments within Nepals Kathmandu Valley World Heritage site. This project involves the structural assessment and restoration of damaged monuments in Kathmandu Durbar Square restored previously with AFCP support.

rows.title: Conservation of Ethnographic Objects at Changu Narayan
rows.description: Changu Narayan is one of the oldest Hindu temple complexes in the Kathmandu valley, but the brick buildings surrounding its ancient (pre-4th-century) courtyard are underutilized and in a state of disrepair. This project continues work begun under an Ambassadors Fund grant in 2011 to restore one of the surrounding buildings for reuse as an ethnographic museum.

rows.title: Preservation of the 11th-Century Richenling Monastery in Halji
rows.description: One of the oldest monasteries in Nepal and the site of an ancient four-sided Vairocana Buddha statue, Richenling monastery is susceptible to irreversible damage both inside and out from water infiltration and rising damp. The project includes preventive conservation measures to protect the building site from flooding and erosion.

rows.title: Conservation of the Dzong Chode Shedup Choepel Ling Gompa in Mustang
rows.description: The Dzong Chode Shedup Choepel Ling Gompa is one of few surviving small, traditional Buddhist monasteries in the Mustang District. This project, which involves the post-earthquake stabilization and conservation of the gompa, directly supports the Embassy's efforts to promote inclusive social and economic development through outreach to overlooked rural communities impacted by the 2015 earthquakes.

rows.title: Conservation and Seismic Strengthening of the 18th-Century Octagonal Krishna Temple in Patan Durbar Square
rows.description: Built through royal patronage in the early 18th-century, the octagonal Krishna Temple stands out for its innovative stone masonry construction. This project includes the development of a model seismic design for earthquake resistance in the conservation of damaged historic stone structures.

rows.title: Conservation of the 8th-Century Lo Gekhar Monastery in Upper Mustang
rows.description: Lo Gekhar is widely considered to be the oldest Tibetan Buddhist monastery in Mustang. This project involves the documentation of the site and surrounding landscape, promotes inclusive socio-economic growth and builds capacities of local institutions and communities to mitigate disaster risks.

rows.title: Conservation and Seismic Strengthening of the 17th-Century Gompa Gang in Mustang
rows.description: Located opposite the Tibetan village of Chuksang, the Temple of Gompa Gang is one of the most important Buddhist monuments in Lower Mustang. This project involves seismic strengthening and the restoration of the building to ensure proper drainage.

rows.title: Conservation of Ancient and Historic Hitis (Stepwells) in the Kathmandu Valley
rows.description: Traditional public stepwells, or hitis, have provided water to communities in the Kathmandu Valley for over 1,500 years. This project involves the conservation and reactivation of a section of the stepwell network to restore its socio-cultural significance and support modern communities adversely impacted by mounting water insecurity.

rows.title: Conservation of Ancient and Historic Buddhist Objects in the Lumbini Museum
rows.description: The Lumbini Museum's collection of ancient and historic Buddhist objects is of exceptional cultural and historical value. The project aims to conserve and restore these objects while building capacity among local conservators and students, thereby setting standards for future conservation efforts in Nepal. Activities include preliminary assessments, establishment of a site lab, detailed conservation and restoration treatments, documentation, inventory creation, and extensive community engagement through exhibitions, memory collection sessions, and media campaigns.

Improve the knowledge base on the prevalence, causes and consequences of modern slavery; select, fund and monitor the most effective and transformational interventions to reduce the prevalence of forced labor of children and adults; disrupt, through sub-awards to the most effective frontline organizations, the norms and systems that sustain forced labor of children and adults; enable, through sub-awards to the most effective frontline organizations, the sustained recovery of children and adult survivors of forced labor; and amplify the impact of PEMS through sharing knowledge and evidence about forced labor of children and adults.

rows.title: "Together for Protection" Project
rows.description: This project will focus on improving protection, prosecution and standards of care in childrens homes (including orphanages where child trafficking is potentially present) by strengthening provincial and local anti-HT and CP structures and mechanisms. To this end, it will work with regulatory bodies at all three levels (national, provincial and local) formed under the Childrens Act 2018 and those to be created through amendment of the Human Trafficking and Transportation (Control) Act (HTTCA) 2007, to develop or update regulatory frameworks governing child institutionalization and human trafficking. The project will also facilitate integration of regulatory frameworks at local levels. Survivor-civic engaged accountability measures will be set up to pressure government and key stakeholders for a pro-active response to ensure that childrens homes are monitored by authorities, operate under minimum standards and integrate adequate protection mechanisms against child trafficking and risks of voluntourism and all persons involved in crimes are prosecuted regardless of their status and position.

rows.title: Maintaining and Advocating for Freedom of Religion or Belief (FoRB)
rows.description: In many societies in Indonesia the lack of understanding of and appreciation for freedom of religion or belief among citizens, civil society organisations and decisions-makers forms an obstacle to free, democratic and just societies. This project strives to increase the respect, protection and promotion of FoRB and to strengthen access to justice for victims of FoRB by empowering civil society actors and human rights defenders, nationally and in four selected regions of intervention. The project includes research, knowledge sharing and training to empower civil society and other actors to advocate for FoRB,

rows.title: Self Help Groups in Makwanpur district, Nepal
rows.description: To explore is the establishment of self-help groups which have proven effective in providing poor communities with low-budget, self-organised options for financial services. Through the mobilization of vulnerable groups to self-help, their regular meetings and the inbuilt capacity building, these groups also have the potential to empower vulnerable groups and promote their political engagement

rows.title: Empoweing the Chepang Community in Makwanpur through finansiel inclusion, skills development and SL
rows.description:
This project is designed to increase the access to agricultural markets and savings and credit among the marginal groups through entrepreneurial endeavors achieving economic empowerment has been implemented targeting rural areas of the Makawanpur district, specifically the Chepang community. The project will improve food security and income as it strengthens agricultural value chains and enables financial inclusion.
The Chepang, an indigenous group from Nepal have been left behind from mainstream development for a long time. They are dependent on subsistence farming and have no access to formal financial services, hence perpetuating cycles of poverty. This initiative strives to solve these challenges by encouraging sustainable farming methods, developing dependable market connections, and providing financial resources through savings and credit programs

rows.title: Empowering local communities  of Rishing Rural Municiplaity for Long-Term Resilience
rows.description: The project is designed to strengthen women’s economic empowerment and sustainable livelihoods for marginalized households in Ramjakot and Pipaltar, Ward No. 5 of Rishing Rural Municipality, Tanahu District. It adopts integrated approaches to promote resilient livelihoods, inclusive economic growth, and strengthened community leadership.  The project is designed to address these gaps by promoting sustainable and climate-smart farming practices, strengthening market linkages, and enhancing financial inclusion through savings and credit mechanisms. These integrated interventions aim to build resilient livelihoods, reduce poverty, and support inclusive rural development.

rows.title: Empowering women through Economic Transformation
rows.description: Det overordnede mål med dette projekt er at fremme økonomisk empowerment for de mest marginaliserede kvinder i Humla gennem bæredygtige levevilkår. Projektet vil gøre sårbare kvinder i stand til at øge deres indkomst ved at gøre dem opmærksomme på deres økonomiske og sociale rettigheder, støtte dem i at organisere sig i landbrugsgrupper og et kooperativ, øge deres produktivitet (gennem træning og materielle input) og øge deres viden om landbrugsforretning og iværksætteri. Tilsvarende vil projektet bidrage til at forbedre den lokale regeringsførelse med det formål at have en ansvarlig lokal regering, der leverer forbedrede lokale tjenester og politikker, der er gunstige for små lokale virksomheder. Mission Øst vil implementere dette projekt i partnerskab med Women Welfare Service (WWS), en lokal NGO i Humla-distriktet.

rows.description: The overall goal of the project is to ensure more resilient livelihoods and to defend the basic human dignity of poor and socially disadvantaged people in the uplands of the far and mid-western regions of Nepal. The specific objective is to strengthen the capacity of poor and socially disadvantaged groups to mobilize their own resources (human, natural, physical, financial); gain access to external resources; and ensure social justice.
